title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Three methods for creating funnel plots for fair visual comparison:

The FunnelPlotR package (quick method)
ggplot2 from scratch (manual method)
plotly + ggplot2 for interactivity

The dataset used is COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv, which contains cumulative COVID-19 cases and deaths by sub-district (kelurahan) as at 31 July 2021.





pacman::p_load(tidyverse, FunnelPlotR, plotly, knitr)

Packages used:



Package
Purpose




tidyverse
Data wrangling & ggplot2 visualisation


FunnelPlotR
High-level funnel plot creation


plotly
Interactive visualisation


knitr
Rendering static HTML tables








covid19 <- read_csv("data/COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv") %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)







Note



The dataset covers 267 sub-districts across five cities in DKI Jakarta. Key columns are Positive (cumulative confirmed cases) and Death (cumulative deaths).


Preview the first few rows:

covid19 %>%
  head(6) %>%
  kable()

The simplest call to funnel_plot() requires only a numerator, denominator, and group variable.

funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Positive,
  denominator = Death,
  group       = `Sub-district`
)








A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 0 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 








Tip



What to note:

group defines the granularity of points (sub-district vs. district or city).
Default data_type is "SR" (standardised ratio).
The plot is automatically adjusted for overdispersion.
Confidence limits shown are 95% and 99.8% by default.







Swap numerator/denominator so we plot the fatality rate (deaths ÷ positives), change data_type to "PR" (proportions), and zoom the axes for readability.

funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group       = `Sub-district`,
  data_type   = "PR",        # proportions instead of standardised ratio
  xrange      = c(0, 6500),  # zoom x-axis
  yrange      = c(0, 0.05)   # zoom y-axis
)








A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 








Tip



data_type = "PR" switches the underlying statistical model to a proportion-based funnel, which is more appropriate when comparing rates (deaths per case) across units of different sizes.






Remove automatic outlier labels and add descriptive axis titles and a plot title.

funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group       = `Sub-district`,
  data_type   = "PR",
  xrange      = c(0, 6500),
  yrange      = c(0, 0.05),
  label       = NA,           # remove outlier labels
  title       = "Cumulative COVID-19 Fatality Rate by Cumulative Total Number of COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  x_label     = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label     = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"
)








A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 






Using FunnelPlotR is convenient, but building the plot manually with ggplot2 gives full control over every visual element.


Compute the death rate and its standard error for each sub-district, then filter out zero-rate sub-districts.

df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate    = Death / Positive,
         rate.se = sqrt((rate * (1 - rate)) / Positive)) %>%
  filter(rate > 0)

glimpse(df)

Rows: 266
Columns: 9
$ `Sub-district ID` <dbl> 3172051003, 3173041007, 3175041005, 3175031003, 3175…
$ City              <fct> JAKARTA UTARA, JAKARTA BARAT, JAKARTA TIMUR, JAKARTA…
$ District          <fct> PADEMANGAN, TAMBORA, KRAMAT JATI, JATINEGARA, CIPAYU…
$ `Sub-district`    <fct> ANCOL, ANGKE, BALE KAMBANG, BALI MESTER, BAMBU APUS,…
$ Positive          <dbl> 1776, 1783, 2049, 827, 2866, 1828, 2541, 3608, 2012,…
$ Recovered         <dbl> 1691, 1720, 1964, 797, 2792, 1757, 2433, 3445, 1937,…
$ Death             <dbl> 26, 29, 31, 13, 27, 26, 37, 68, 38, 52, 72, 28, 25, …
$ rate              <dbl> 0.014639640, 0.016264722, 0.015129331, 0.015719468, …
$ rate.se           <dbl> 0.002849976, 0.002995620, 0.002696674, 0.004325393, …


Compute the weighted grand mean rate (inverse-variance weighting):

fit.mean <- weighted.mean(df$rate, 1 / df$rate.se^2)
fit.mean

[1] 0.01496959






Generate a sequence of denominator values from 1 to the maximum, then compute lower/upper funnel limits at both confidence levels.

number.seq   <- seq(1, max(df$Positive), 1)

number.ll95  <- fit.mean - 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ul95  <- fit.mean + 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ll999 <- fit.mean - 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ul999 <- fit.mean + 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)

dfCI <- data.frame(
  number.seq, fit.mean,
  number.ll95, number.ul95,
  number.ll999, number.ul999
)

head(dfCI)

  number.seq   fit.mean number.ll95 number.ul95 number.ll999 number.ul999
1          1 0.01496959 -0.22303528   0.2529745   -0.3845386    0.4144778
2          2 0.01496959 -0.15332527   0.1832645   -0.2675254    0.2974645
3          3 0.01496959 -0.12244259   0.1523818   -0.2156866    0.2456257
4          4 0.01496959 -0.10403285   0.1339720   -0.1847845    0.2147237
5          5 0.01496959 -0.09146942   0.1214086   -0.1636959    0.1936351
6          6 0.01496959 -0.08219549   0.1121347   -0.1481289    0.1780681








Note



Why these multipliers?



Limit
Multiplier
Interpretation




95% CI
1.96
~2 SD from mean; “common cause” variation


99.9% CI
3.29
~3.3 SD from mean; strong evidence of outlier









Layer the data points, funnel bands, and a mean reference line.

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate)) +
  
  # Data points
  geom_point(aes(label = `Sub-district`),
             alpha = 0.4) +
  
  # 95% lower limit (dashed)
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ll95),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  
  # 95% upper limit (dashed)
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ul95),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  
  # 99.9% lower limit (solid)
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ll999),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  
  # 99.9% upper limit (solid)
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ul999),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  
  # Grand mean reference line
  geom_hline(data = dfCI,
             aes(yintercept = fit.mean),
             size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  
  # Zoom y-axis without clipping CI lines
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 0.05)) +
  
  # CI band labels
  annotate("text", x = 1,   y = -0.13, label = "95%",
           size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = 4.5, y = -0.18, label = "99%",
           size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  
  # Titles
  ggtitle("Cumulative Fatality Rate by Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  xlab("Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  ylab("Cumulative Fatality Rate") +
  
  theme_light() +
  theme(
    plot.title        = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position   = c(0.91, 0.85),
    legend.title      = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.text       = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.background = element_rect(colour = "grey60", linetype = "dotted"),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.3, "cm")
  )

p














Tip



Reading the funnel plot:

Points within the 95% band: variation is within expected statistical noise.
Points between the 95% and 99.9% bands: moderate evidence of unusual performance.
Points outside the 99.9% band: strong evidence the sub-district’s rate differs from the overall average.







Wrap the ggplot2 object with ggplotly() to enable hover tooltips showing the sub-district name, case count, and fatality rate.

fp_ggplotly <- ggplotly(p,
  tooltip = c("label", "x", "y"))

fp_ggplotly











Tip



Hover over any point to see the sub-district name, cumulative positive cases, and fatality rate. This makes it easy to identify and investigate outlying sub-districts without cluttering the static chart with labels.















Method
Pros
Cons




FunnelPlotR
Fast, handles overdispersion automatically
Limited customisation


ggplot2 (manual)
Full control over every visual element
More code required


plotly + ggplot2
Adds interactivity with minimal extra code
Requires label aesthetic on geom_point



A funnel plot is most valuable when we need to compare many units (e.g., hospitals, districts) while accounting for the fact that smaller units have more variability — the narrowing funnel shape naturally encodes this statistical reality.




Kam, T.S. (2023). Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Setting Up

Packages

pacman::p_load(ggstatsplot, tidyverse)

ggstatsplot is built on top of ggplot2, it follows the APA standard for reporting statistics, which means the output is actually publication-ready.


Load the Data

exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam)

Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…


The dataset has 322 students with scores in English, Maths, and Science, plus demographic info like Class, Gender, and Race.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
One-Sample Test with gghistostats()
Question: Is the average English score significantly different from 60?

set.seed(1234)

gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "bayes",       # using Bayesian inference
  test.value = 60,      # testing against this benchmark
  xlab = "English scores"
)














Note



type = "bayes" runs a Bayesian one-sample t-test rather than the classic frequentist version.



log_e(BF₀₁) = −31.45, this is the log Bayes Factor in favour of H0. It’s massively negative, meaning the evidence is overwhelmingly against H0 (that the mean = 60). Flipping it: BF₁₀ is enormous, so we have extreme evidence that the true mean is not 60.
δ̂ posterior difference = 7.16, the standardised effect size
CI⁹⁵% [5.54, 8.75], 95% credible interval for that effect
r_Cauchy = 0.71, the prior scale used for the Bayesian test

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Understanding the Bayes Factor
A Bayes Factor (BF₁₀) compares two hypotheses:

H0: the null hypothesis (e.g., mean = 60)
H1: the alternate hypothesis (e.g., mean ≠ 60)

\[BF_{10} = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid H_1)}{P(\text{data} \mid H_0)}\] One practical way to estimate it is using the Schwarz/BIC criterion. It’s a rougher approximation but much easier to compute.

Interpreting BF₁₀ Values



Bayes Factor
Interpretation




> 100
Extreme evidence for H1


30 – 100
Very strong evidence for H1


10 – 30
Strong evidence for H1


3 – 10
Moderate evidence for H1


1 – 3
Anecdotal evidence for H1


1
No evidence either way


1/3 – 1
Anecdotal evidence for H0


1/10 – 1/3
Moderate evidence for H0


< 1/10
Strong evidence for H0

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Two-Sample Mean Test with ggbetweenstats()
Question: Do Male and Female students score differently in Maths?

Testing for normality

Step 1: Test normality for each gender group


exam %>%
  group_by(GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    shapiro_p = shapiro.test(MATHS)$p.value,
    n = n()
  )

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  GENDER    shapiro_p     n
  <chr>         <dbl> <int>
1 Female 0.000000160    170
2 Male   0.0000000627   152



Step 2: Visualise the distribution


ggplot(exam, aes(sample = MATHS, color = GENDER)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  facet_wrap(~GENDER) +
  labs(title = "Q-Q Plot of Maths Scores by Gender")









Step 3: Choose test based on normality result

If Shapiro-Wilk p > 0.05 → normality holds → use “p” (parametric, Student’s t-test)
If Shapiro-Wilk p < 0.05 → normality violated → use “np” (non-parametric, Mann-Whitney U)



ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "np",        # non-parametric test (Mann-Whitney U)
  messages = FALSE
)








type = "np" was used here for a non-parametric test, as the data violates normality assumption based on the Shapiro-Wilk’s test. The plot shows the data distribution (violin + boxplot + points) alongside the test statistic.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
One-Way ANOVA with ggbetweenstats()
The same function handles ANOVA when there are more than 2 groups. Here I’m checking if English scores differ across racial groups.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = RACE,
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "p",                      # parametric (standard ANOVA)
  mean.ci = TRUE,                  # show confidence intervals for means
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,     # run post-hoc pairwise tests
  pairwise.display = "s",          # "s" = show only significant pairs
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",         # False Discovery Rate correction
  messages = FALSE
)









Options for pairwise.display



Value
What it shows




"s"
Only significant pairwise comparisons


"ns"
Only non-significant comparisons


"all"
All pairwise comparisons





Which test runs under the hood?
ggbetweenstats is smart about picking the right test depending on type and group count:










Scenario
type = "p"
type = "np"
type = "r" (robust)
type = "bayes"




2 groups
Student’s t
Mann-Whitney U
Yuen’s t
Bayes t-test


3+ groups
Fisher’s ANOVA
Kruskal-Wallis
Trimmed means ANOVA
Bayes ANOVA



Post-hoc pairwise comparisons are adjusted. FDR ("fdr") is a good choice to control false discoveries when there are many comparisons.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Correlation Test with ggscatterstats()
Question: Is there a significant correlation between Maths and English scores?

ggscatterstats(
  data = exam,
  x = MATHS,
  y = ENGLISH,
  marginal = FALSE    # set TRUE to add marginal distributions on the sides
)








The scatterplot comes with:

A fitted regression line
Confidence band
Correlation coefficient and p-value right in the subtitle

Setting marginal = TRUE would add density/histogram plots along the axes — useful but can be visually busy.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Association Test with ggbarstats()
Question: Is there a significant association between Maths performance bands and Gender?
First, I need to bin the continuous Maths score into categories:

exam1 <- exam %>%
  mutate(
    MATHS_bins = cut(
      MATHS,
      breaks = c(0, 60, 75, 85, 100),
      labels = c("Fail (<60)", "Pass (60-75)", "Good (75-85)", "Excellent (85+)")
    )
  )

Then plot:

ggbarstats(
  data = exam1,
  x = MATHS_bins,
  y = GENDER
)








This runs a chi-square test of independence and displays:

Stacked bar proportions by gender
Chi-square statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value
Cramér’s V (effect size)

The cut() function is important here — I defined breaks at 0, 60, 75, 85, 100 to create meaningful academic performance bands.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
When creating animations, the plot does not actually move. Instead, many individual plots are built and then stitched together as movie frames, like an old-school flip book or cartoon. Each frame is a different plot when conveying motion, built using a relevant subset of the aggregate data. The subset drives the flow of the animation when stitched back together.
Before diving in, it is important to understand some key concepts:

Frame: Each frame represents a different point in time or category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph update to reflect the new data.
Animation Attributes: Settings that control how the animation behaves — for example, the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start from the current frame or the beginning.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading the R Packages
The following packages are used in this chapter:

plotly for plotting interactive statistical graphs
gganimate: a ggplot2 extension for animated statistical graphs
gifski converts video frames to GIF animations using efficient cross-frame palettes and temporal dithering
gapminder provides the country_colors colour scheme used in the plots
tidyverse for data wrangling, transformation, and static graphics


pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing the Data
The Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation Excel workbook is used throughout this chapter.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(col, as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))







Note



Things to learn from the code chunk above:

read_xls() from readxl imports the Excel worksheet.
mutate(across()) converts the Country and Continent character columns to factors. This replaces the deprecated mutate_each_() and mutate_at() approaches from older dplyr versions.
mutate(Year = as.integer(Year)) converts the Year field to integer type.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation: gganimate Methods
gganimate extends the grammar of graphics implemented by ggplot2 to include animation. It provides a range of new grammar classes that can be added to the plot object:







Function
Purpose




transition_*()
Defines how data spreads across time


view_*()
Defines how positional scales change during animation


shadow_*()
Defines how data from other time points is displayed


enter_*()/exit_*()
Defines how new/old data appears and disappears


ease_aes()
Defines how aesthetics are eased during transitions




Building a Static Population Bubble Plot
The basic ggplot2 functions are used to create a static bubble plot first. This is the foundation before animating.

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                      size = Population,
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}',
       x = '% Aged',
       y = '% Young')










Building the Animated Bubble Plot
Two gganimate functions are added to bring the plot to life:

transition_time(): creates transitions through distinct states in time (i.e. Year)
ease_aes(): controls easing of aesthetics during transitions. The default is linear, other options include quadratic, cubic, sine, elastic, bounce, and more.


ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                      size = Population,
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}',
       x = '% Aged',
       y = '% Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes('linear')

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation: plotly
In Plotly R, both ggplotly() and plot_ly() support key frame animations through the frame argument/aesthetic. They also support an ids argument/aesthetic to ensure smooth transitions between objects with the same id (facilitating object constancy).

Building an Animated Bubble Plot: ggplotly() Method
The animated bubble plot produced with ggplotly() includes a play/pause button and a slider component for controlling the animation.

gg <- ggplot(globalPop,
       aes(x = Old,
           y = Young,
           size = Population,
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged',
       y = '% Young') +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')

ggplotly(gg)











Note



Things to learn from the code chunk above:

A static bubble plot is built with ggplot2 and saved as the R object gg.
ggplotly() converts the R graphic object into an animated SVG object.
theme(legend.position = 'none') is required to suppress the legend.
Using show.legend = FALSE inside geom_point() does not remove it in plotly.





Building an Animated Bubble Plot: plot_ly() Method
The plot_ly() method provides a more direct plotly interface, colouring bubbles by Continent and using hoverinfo to show country names on hover.

bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old,
          y = ~Young,
          size = ~Population,
          color = ~Continent,
          sizes = c(2, 100),
          frame = ~Year,
          text = ~Country,
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = 'scatter',
          mode = 'markers'
          ) %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)

bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
Histogram of Maths Scores
Here, you can compare the difference between R Graphics, the core graphical function of Base R, and ggplot plotting a histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, color = "black", fill = "lightgrey") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of exam_data$MATHS",
       x = "exam_data$MATHS",
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw()











As we can see, the difference is obvious. As quoted from Hadley Wickham:

The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics - ggplot2 methods
text:
Grammar of Graphics
The ggplot2 follows a layered grammer of graphics, the 7 layers can be described as follow:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics: It take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets: Split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: Statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate: It defines the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes: Modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
This chapter introduces interactive data visualization in R, covering three major approaches:

ggiraph: makes ggplot2 graphics interactive (tooltips, hover, click)
plotly: rich interactive charts via plot_ly() and ggplotly()
crosstalk: linked brushing and filtering across multiple widgets

We apply these techniques to the Superstore sales dataset, a transactional retail dataset covering orders from 2022–2025 across the United States, with fields for customer segment, product category, region, sales, discount, and profit.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
Tooltip: Profit Margin by Sub-Category
We start with a simple bar chart showing average profit margin per sub-category, with a rich tooltip revealing the full picture.

# Summarise by sub-category
subcat_summary <- superstore |>  # |> is the pipe operator, which takes the output of one expression and passes it as the first argument to the next function.
  group_by(`Sub-Category`, Category) |>
  summarise(
    TotalSales   = sum(Sales),
    TotalProfit  = sum(Profit),
    AvgMargin    = mean(ProfitMargin),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "<b>", `Sub-Category`, "</b><br>",
      "Category: ", Category, "<br>",
      "Total Sales: $", scales::comma(round(TotalSales, 0)), "<br>",
      "Total Profit: $", scales::comma(round(TotalProfit, 0)), "<br>",
      "Avg Margin: ", scales::percent(AvgMargin, accuracy = 0.1)
    ),
    fill_colour = case_when(
      AvgMargin >  0.15 ~ "High",
      AvgMargin >  0    ~ "Positive",
      TRUE              ~ "Loss-making"
    )
  )

p_bar <- ggplot(subcat_summary,
                aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, AvgMargin),
                    y = AvgMargin,
                    fill = fill_colour)) +
  geom_col_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text,
        data_id = `Sub-Category`),
    width = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("High" = "#2196F3", "Positive" = "#81C784", "Loss-making" = "#E57373"),
    name = "Margin tier"
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Average Profit Margin by Sub-Category",
    subtitle = "Hover for sales and profit details",
    x = NULL, y = "Average Profit Margin"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

girafe(
  ggobj       = p_bar,
  width_svg   = 8,
  height_svg  = 8 * 0.75
)











ImportantTakeaway: Tables and Bookcases are destroying margins



Tables and Bookcases both have negative average profit margins, every sale in these sub-categories on average loses money. Paper, by contrast, have the highest profit margin, even if the total sales/revenue is not as high as Tables and Bookcases.

text:
Hover Effect with data_id
data_id let us highlight all elements that share the same group. When we hover over one bar, all bars in the same category light up together, which can be very useful for comparing within-group behaviour.

subcatplot <- superstore |>
  group_by(Category, `Sub-Category`) |>
  summarise(TotalSales = sum(Sales), .groups = "drop")

p_grouped <- ggplot(subcatplot,
                    aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, TotalSales),
                        y = TotalSales,
                        fill = Category)) +
  geom_col_interactive(
    aes(tooltip  = paste0(`Sub-Category`, ": $", scales::comma(round(TotalSales, 0))),
        data_id  = Category),
    width = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Total Sales by Sub-Category",
    subtitle = "Hover to highlight all sub-categories within the same product category",
    x = NULL, y = "Total Sales",
    fill = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

girafe(
  ggobj      = p_grouped,
  width_svg  = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.75,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill-opacity: 1; stroke: #333; stroke-width: 1.5px;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity: 0.15;")
  )
)











TipHow opts_hover_inv works



opts_hover_inv controls the opacity of non-hovered elements. Setting opacity: 0.15 makes everything else fade out, directing attention to the hovered group.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
Another highligting feature of ggiraph is by assigning the same data_id (e.g. Sub-Category) to elements in two side-by-side plots, hovering in one automatically highlights the corresponding point in the other.

sc <- superstore |>
  group_by(`Sub-Category`, Category) |>
  summarise(
    TotalSales  = sum(Sales),
    TotalProfit = sum(Profit),
    AvgDiscount = mean(Discount),
    .groups     = "drop"
  )

p_left <- ggplot(sc, aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, TotalSales), y = TotalSales, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = paste0(`Sub-Category`, "\nSales: $", scales::comma(round(TotalSales))),
        data_id = `Sub-Category`),
    width = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Total Sales", 
       subtitle = "Linked views: hover on a sub-category in either plot to highlight it in both.",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_right <- ggplot(sc, aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, TotalSales), y = TotalProfit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = paste0(`Sub-Category`, "\nProfit: $", scales::comma(round(TotalProfit))),
        data_id = `Sub-Category`),
    width = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Total Profit", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y  = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

girafe(
  code       = print(p_left + p_right),
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 5,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill-opacity:1; stroke:#333; stroke-width:1.5px;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.1;")
  )
)











ImportantTakeaway: The Sales ≠ Profit trap



Chairs (#1 in sales) and Tables (#4) look similar on the left but differs largely on the right. Tables actually lose money at scale. Coordinated views make this contrast instantly visible in a way a single chart cannot.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
plot_ly(): Sales vs Profit Scatter

order_summary <- superstore |>
  group_by(`Order ID`, Segment, Category) |>
  summarise(
    Sales   = sum(Sales),
    Profit  = sum(Profit),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

plot_ly(
  data   = order_summary,
  x      = ~Sales,
  y      = ~Profit,
  color  = ~Segment,
  symbol = ~Category,
  type   = "scatter",
  mode   = "markers",
  marker = list(size = 5, opacity = 0.6),
  text   = ~paste0("Segment: ", Segment,
                   "<br>Category: ", Category,
                   "<br>Sales: $", round(Sales, 2),
                   "<br>Profit: $", round(Profit, 2)),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) |>
  layout(
    title  = "Order-Level Sales vs Profit by Segment and Category",
    xaxis  = list(title = "Order Sales ($)", type = "log"),
    yaxis  = list(title = "Order Profit ($)"),
    legend = list(title = list(text = "Segment"))
  )



Click legend items to show/hide segments. Hover for details.








Note



The log scale on the x-axis is intentional, due to the span on the sales amount. On a linear scale, most points would crowd into the left corner, distorting the patterns.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
ggplotly(): Monthly Sales Trend
Writing a normal ggplot, then wrap it in ggplotly() to get hover tooltips, zoom, and pan.

monthly <- superstore |>
  group_by(Year, Month, Category) |>
  summarise(Sales = sum(Sales), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(YearMonth = paste(Year, Month, sep = "-"))

p_trend <- ggplot(monthly,
                  aes(x = Month, y = Sales, colour = Category, group = interaction(Year, Category),
                      text = paste0(Month, " ", Year,
                                    "<br>Category: ", Category,
                                    "<br>Sales: $", scales::comma(round(Sales))))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~Year, ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category and Year",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Monthly Sales",
    colour   = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggplotly(p_trend, tooltip = "text")



Hover over a point to see exact monthly figures. Use the legend to toggle categories.








TipWhen to use ggplotly() vs plot_ly()



Use ggplotly() when we already have a ggplot2 plot and just want interactivity quickly. Use plot_ly() when we need fine-grained control over plotly-specific features like 3D plots, animations, or custom hover templates.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly
Use highlight_key() to create a shared data object, build two separate plots, then can link them with subplot(). Clicking a point in one panel highlights the corresponding points in the other.

order_view <- superstore |>
  group_by(`Order ID`, Segment, Region) |>
  summarise(
    Sales    = sum(Sales),
    Profit   = sum(Profit),
    Discount = mean(Discount),
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

d_shared <- highlight_key(order_view)

p1 <- ggplot(d_shared, aes(x = Sales, y = Profit, colour = Segment,
                            text = paste0("Order: ", `Order ID`,
                                          "<br>Segment: ", Segment,
                                          "<br>Sales: $", round(Sales),
                                          "<br>Profit: $", round(Profit)))) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Sales vs Profit", x = "Sales (log)", y = "Profit") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

p2 <- ggplot(d_shared, aes(x = Discount, y = Profit, colour = Segment,
                            text = paste0("Order: ", `Order ID`,
                                          "<br>Discount: ", percent(Discount),
                                          "<br>Profit: $", round(Profit)))) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Discount vs Profit", x = "Discount Rate", y = "Profit") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

subplot(
  ggplotly(p1, tooltip = "text"),
  ggplotly(p2, tooltip = "text"),
  nrows          = 1,
  shareY         = TRUE,
  titleX         = TRUE
) |>
  highlight(on = "plotly_click", off = "plotly_deselect") |>
  layout(title = "Coordinated View: Sales vs Profit & Discount vs Profit")



Click any point to highlight the same order across both panels.








ImportantTakeaway: Discounts beyond 40% are almost universally unprofitable



The right panel (Discount vs Profit) tells that orders with discounts above 40% are generally non-profitable. This is a structural pricing problem.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
text:
Linked Brushing: Scatter + Table
The final pattern is the most powerful: select points on the scatter, and the table below automatically filters to show only those rows.

brush_data <- superstore |>
  select(`Order ID`, `Customer Name`, Segment, Region, Category,
         `Sub-Category`, Sales, Discount, Profit) |>
  mutate(
    Sales    = round(Sales, 2),
    Profit   = round(Profit, 2)
  )

d_brush <- highlight_key(brush_data)

scatter <- ggplot(d_brush, aes(x = Sales, y = Profit,
                                colour = Category,
                                text   = paste0(`Sub-Category`,
                                                "<br>", `Customer Name`,
                                                "<br>$", Sales, " / $", Profit))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title  = "Click or drag to select — table below updates automatically",
    x      = "Sales (log scale)",
    y      = "Profit ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

gg_scatter <- highlight(
  ggplotly(scatter, tooltip = "text"),
  on  = "plotly_selected",
  off = "plotly_deselect"
)

crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(12),
  gg_scatter,
  DT::datatable(
    d_brush,
    class    = "compact",
    rownames = FALSE,
    options  = list(pageLength = 8, scrollX = TRUE)
  ) |>
    DT::formatCurrency(c("Sales", "Profit"))
)













Drag to select a region on the scatter plot — the table updates to match.








NoteHow crosstalk works under the hood



highlight() is a function of plotly package. It sets a variety of options for brushing (i.e., highlighting) multiple plots. These options are primarily designed for linking multiple plotly graphs, and may not behave as expected when linking plotly to another htmlwidget package via crosstalk. In some cases, other htmlwidgets will respect these options, such as persistent selection in leaflet.

text:
Methodological Lessons







Concept
Key Point




geom_*_interactive()
Drop-in replacement for ggplot2 geoms; tooltip/data_id/onclick are the three hooks


girafe()
Finalises the SVG widget; opts_tooltip(), opts_hover(), opts_hover_inv() control styling


data_id
The bridge for coordinated views in ggiraph, using the same column in both plots


ggplotly()
Fastest path to interactivity, passing tooltip = "text" for custom hover text


highlight_key()
Shared data pointer, binding multiple widgets to it for linked brushing without Shiny


subplot()
Places plotly charts side-by-side with optional shared axes


DT::datatable()
Powerful for exploration; filter = "top" and Buttons extension are useful

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualization with R
section: Business Insights from the Superstore Data
text:
Business Insights from the Superstore Data

Tables and Bookcases are money-losers. Despite Table’s ranking as the 4th highest revenue sub-category, it runs at a negative average profit margin. Discounting in Furniture is likely the culprit.
Discounts over 40% are almost never profitable. The discount–profit scatter shows a clear inflection point around 40%. Any discount above that level correlates strongly with negative profit.
Technology has the best margin quality. Despite not being the top revenue category, Technology generates the highest total profit, where it was driven by Copiers and Accessories, both of which have strong positive margins.
The West region leads profitability across all three customer segments. The Central region is the most volatile.
Sales volume alone is a misleading KPI. Chairs are the #1 sub-category by revenue but sit in the middle of profit rankings. Any sales dashboard that shows only revenue is hiding the costs.

title: 4a. Visualizing Distribution
text:
Two new statistical graphic methods for visualizing distribution will be covered in this section.

Ridgeline plots: Show distributions for multiple groups stacked along a shared axis, using ggridges.
Raincloud plots: Combine a half-density, boxplot, and dot plot into one layered graphic, using ggdist.

Both reveal richer distributional shape such as skewness, multimodality, and tail behaviour.





pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)








Package
Role




ggridges
Ridgeline plots via geom_density_ridges() and variants


ggdist
Half-eye, dot plots, uncertainty visualisation


ggthemes
Extra themes, e.g. theme_economist() for publication-ready look


colorspace
Colour manipulation via lighten(), darken(), palette tools


tidyverse
Data wrangling (dplyr, tidyr), and base plotting (ggplot2)







exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam)

Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…


The dataset has 322 students across 9 classes (3A–3I) with scores in English, Maths, and Science. Variables CLASS, GENDER, and RACE serve as grouping factors throughout this chapter.




A ridgeline plot (formerly called a Joyplot) shows the distribution of a numeric variable for several groups, stacked with a slight overlap along the y-axis.

When to use ridgeline plots:

5 or more groups to compare (if we have fewer groups, use violin/boxplot instead)
Existence of natural ordering to the groups (e.g. class ranking)
Reveal shape/distribution differences across groups, not just means


In the Exam dataset used here, 9 classes (3A–3I) are a perfect candidate.


Distribution of English scores across all 9 classes using geom_density_ridges(). The classes are ordered 3A to 3I (best to weakest by class assignment).

The plotThe code










Figure 1: English score distributions by class - basic ridgeline plot.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth      = 3.4,
    fill           = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color          = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "English Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()




Key arguments to understand:

scale = 3: It controls how much ridges overlap (higher = more overlap)
rel_min_height = 0.01: It trims tails below 1% of the peak height, avoiding long flat tails
bandwidth = 3.4: It controls the kernel density smoother width. The higher, the smoother.


Takeaway: Class 3A and 3B have distributions clustered at high scores (70-90), while 3H and 3I are concentrated at low scores (20-50). Notably, the shape differs as well, 3I shows a narrow, peaked distribution (homogeneously weak), while middle classes like 3E-3F show broader, flatter distributions indicating mixed ability.




Instead of a single flat fill colour, we can let the fill vary along the score axis using geom_density_ridges_gradient(). This adds a visual cue for score magnitude.

The plotThe code










Figure 2: Maths score distributions with fill colour varying along the score axis.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = MATHS,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = after_stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()





Takeaway: The gradient fill immediately communicates which classes cluster at high vs low scores without needing to read axis values carefully. However, fill and alpha transparency cannot be used together with gradient fills, which is a technical limitation to take note.


Code: after_stat(x) is the modern replacement for the deprecated stat(x) syntax used in the course notes. Both map the computed x-axis value onto the fill aesthetic.




Beside providing additional geom objects to support the need to plot ridgeline plot, ggridges package also provides a stat function called stat_density_ridges() that replaces stat_density() of ggplot2.

The plotThe code










Figure 3: Science score ridgelines coloured by tail probability (ECDF).








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = SCIENCE,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name      = "Tail\nProbability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Science Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()





Takeaway: The expression 0.5 - abs(0.5 - ecdf) maps values closest to the median to the highest fill value, and tail extremes to the lowest. Dark regions are the rare extreme scores. Bright central bands show where most students fall. This is especially useful for spotting which classes have fat tails (more outliers) vs tight central distributions.







Important



Always include calc_ecdf = TRUE inside stat_density_ridges() when using ECDF-based colour mapping, the plot will silently produce incorrect results without it.





We can colour the ridgeline by quartile bands to make Q1, median, and Q3 immediately visible within each class’s density shape.

Quartile BandThe codeProbability BandThe code










Figure 4: English score ridgelines coloured by quartile band.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom           = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf      = TRUE,
    quantiles      = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()











Figure 5: English scores with 2.5% and 97.5% tail regions highlighted in red/blue.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name   = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("Bottom 2.5%", "Middle 95%", "Top 2.5%")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()








A Raincloud plot overlays three visual layers to give a complete picture of the distribution:








Layer
Geom
What it shows




Half-density (“cloud”)
stat_halfeye()
Overall shape, skewness, modes


Boxplot
geom_boxplot()
Median, IQR, outliers


Dot plot (“rain”)
stat_dots()
Individual data points, sample size




The plotThe code










Figure 6: Raincloud plot: Maths scores by Gender.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = GENDER,
           y = MATHS)) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust        = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width        = 0,
    point_colour  = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width         = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side          = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth      = 0.5,
    dotsize       = 1.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()







Kam, T.S. (2023). Visualising Distribution.

  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()





Takeaway: The gradient fill immediately communicates which classes cluster at high vs low scores without needing to read axis values carefully. However, fill and alpha transparency cannot be used together with gradient fills, which is a technical limitation to take note.


Code: after_stat(x) is the modern replacement for the deprecated stat(x) syntax used in the course notes. Both map the computed x-axis value onto the fill aesthetic.




Beside providing additional geom objects to support the need to plot ridgeline plot, ggridges package also provides a stat function called stat_density_ridges() that replaces stat_density() of ggplot2.

The plotThe code










Figure 3: Science score ridgelines coloured by tail probability (ECDF).








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = SCIENCE,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name      = "Tail\nProbability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Science Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()





Takeaway: The expression 0.5 - abs(0.5 - ecdf) maps values closest to the median to the highest fill value, and tail extremes to the lowest. Dark regions are the rare extreme scores. Bright central bands show where most students fall. This is especially useful for spotting which classes have fat tails (more outliers) vs tight central distributions.







Important



Always include calc_ecdf = TRUE inside stat_density_ridges() when using ECDF-based colour mapping, the plot will silently produce incorrect results without it.





We can colour the ridgeline by quartile bands to make Q1, median, and Q3 immediately visible within each class’s density shape.

Quartile BandThe codeProbability BandThe code










Figure 4: English score ridgelines coloured by quartile band.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom           = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf      = TRUE,
    quantiles      = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()











Figure 5: English scores with 2.5% and 97.5% tail regions highlighted in red/blue.








ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name   = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("Bottom 2.5%", "Middle 95%", "Top 2.5%")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()

text: Kam, T.S. (2023). Visualising Distribution.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Raw summary statistics (e.g. a mean value) are point estimates, they tell us nothing about how confident we should be in that number. Communicating uncertainty helps us make better decisions and avoids misleading conclusions.
These will be covered below:

Plot standard error bars using ggplot2.
Plot confidence interval error bars using ggplot2.
Create interactive error bars by combining ggplot2, plotly, and DT.
Build advanced uncertainty visuals using the ggdist package.
Create Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs) using the ungeviz package.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Key Terms








Term
What it measures
Formula




Standard Deviation (SD)
Spread of individual observations
\(s\)


Standard Error (SE)
Uncertainty of the sample mean
\(SE = s / \sqrt{n-1}\)


Confidence Interval (CI)
Plausible range for the true mean
\(\bar{x} \pm z \times SE\)




Important: Do not confuse the uncertainty of a point estimate (SE / CI) with the variation in the sample (SD). They answer different questions.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Installing and Loading Packages

pacman::p_load(
  plotly,       # Interactive plots
  crosstalk,    # Cross-widget interactions (linked brushing/filtering)
  DT,           # Interactive HTML tables
  ggdist,       # Distribution and uncertainty visualisation
  ggridges,     # Ridgeline plots
  colorspace,   # Colour palette utilities
  gganimate,    # Animated plots
  tidyverse     # Core data science packages (ggplot2, dplyr, etc.)
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Data Import
We use Exam_data.csv, which contains exam scores (Maths, English, Science) for primary school students, along with demographic variables such as RACE, GENDER, and CLASS.

exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam)

Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Deriving Summary Statistics
To plot error bars we first need to compute, per group:

n: number of observations
mean: average Maths score
sd: standard deviation
se: standard error = \(sd / \sqrt{n-1}\)


my_sum <- exam %>%
  group_by(RACE) %>%
  summarise(
    n    = n(),
    mean = mean(MATHS),
    sd   = sd(MATHS)
  ) %>%
  mutate(se = sd / sqrt(n - 1))

knitr::kable(my_sum, format = "html", digits = 2,
             caption = "Summary statistics of Maths scores by Race")


Summary statistics of Maths scores by Race


RACE
n
mean
sd
se




Chinese
193
76.51
15.69
1.13


Indian
12
60.67
23.35
7.04


Malay
108
57.44

2.04

9
69.67
10.72
3.79





Reading the table:

Chinese pupils have the largest group (\(n = 193\)) and the smallest SE (\(\approx 1.13\)), which means we have a precise estimate of their mean.
Indian pupils have the smallest group (\(n = 12\)) and a very large SE (\(\approx 7.04\)), reflecting much greater uncertainty about their true mean.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Standard Error Bars
Standard error bars extend one SE above and below the mean. They communicate how precisely the sample mean estimates the population mean. Narrower bars mean more precision.

ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x = RACE, ymin = mean - se, ymax = mean + se),
    width     = 0.2,
    colour    = "black",
    alpha     = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = RACE, y = mean),
    stat  = "identity",
    color = "red",
    size  = 1.5,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Standard Error of Mean Maths Score by Race",
    x     = "Race",
    y     = "Mean Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 1: Standard error bars of mean Maths score by race. Red dots = sample means; bars = ±1 SE.





What to notice:

The Chinese group has a very tight error bar (large sample, high precision).
The Indian and Others groups have much wider bars (small samples, low precision).
The visual inequality in bar widths reinforces why sample size matters when comparing groups.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
95% Confidence Interval Bars
A 95% CI is computed as \(\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times SE\). It means: if we repeated this study many times, ~95% of the constructed intervals would contain the true population mean.
The races are reordered by descending mean so the plot is easier to read.

ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      x    = reorder(RACE, -mean),
      ymin = mean - 1.96 * se,
      ymax = mean + 1.96 * se
    ),
    width     = 0.2,
    colour    = "black",
    alpha     = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = reorder(RACE, -mean), y = mean),
    stat  = "identity",
    color = "red",
    size  = 1.5,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    x     = "Race",
    y     = "Mean Maths Score",
    title = "95% Confidence Interval of Mean Maths Score by Race"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 2: 95% confidence intervals of mean Maths score by race, sorted by descending mean.





Key difference from SE bars: The multiplier changes the interval width:



Confidence Level
Multiplier
Interpretation




~68%
±1.00 SE
Equivalent to ±1 SE bars


95%
±1.96 SE
Standard CI in most research


99%
±2.58 SE
More conservative, wider bars

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Interactive Error Bars (99% CI)
By combining plotly (interactive chart), DT (interactive table), and crosstalk (linked selection), we can build a dashboard where clicking a row in the table highlights the corresponding bar in the plot.

shared_df = SharedData$new(my_sum)

bscols(widths = c(4,8),
       ggplotly((ggplot(shared_df) +
                   geom_errorbar(aes(
                     x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
                     ymin=mean-2.58*se, 
                     ymax=mean+2.58*se), 
                     width=0.2, 
                     colour="black", 
                     alpha=0.9, 
                     size=0.5) +
                   geom_point(aes(
                     x=RACE, 
                     y=mean, 
                     text = paste("Race:", `RACE`, 
                                  "<br>N:", `n`,
                                  "<br>Avg. Scores:", round(mean, digits = 2),
                                  "<br>95% CI:[", 
                                  round((mean-2.58*se), digits = 2), ",",
                                  round((mean+2.58*se), digits = 2),"]")),
                     stat="identity", 
                     color="red", 
                     size = 1.5, 
                     alpha=1) + 
                   xlab("Race") + 
                   ylab("Average Scores") + 
                   theme_minimal() + 
                   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(
                     angle = 45, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1)) +
                   ggtitle("99% Confidence interval of average /<br>maths scores by race")), 
                tooltip = "text"), 
       DT::datatable(shared_df, 
                     rownames = FALSE, 
                     class="compact", 
                     width="100%", 
                     options = list(pageLength = 10,
                                    scrollX=T), 
                     colnames = c("No. of pupils", 
                                  "Avg Scores",
                                  "Std Dev",
                                  "Std Error")) %>%
         formatRound(columns=c('mean', 'sd', 'se'),
                     digits=2))















Why interactivity helps: Hovering over a point reveals exact values that are hard to read off a static plot. The linked table lets you filter by group without redrawing the chart.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
stat_pointinterval() Point + Interval
stat_pointinterval() shows a point estimate (default: mean) together with multiple nested intervals (default: 66% and 95%).

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval() +
  labs(
    title    = "Visualising Confidence Intervals of Mean Maths Score",
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot",
    x        = "Race",
    y        = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 3: Mean and multiple confidence intervals of Maths scores by race using stat_pointinterval().





The thick inner line = 66% CI; the thin outer line = 95% CI. This nested interval design lets you read two confidence levels at once.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
stat_pointinterval() Median + Quantile Interval
We can switch to the median as the point summary and use quantile intervals (qi) instead of the default. This is more appropriate for skewed distributions.

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval(
    .width    = 0.95,
    .point    = median,
    .interval = qi
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Visualising Confidence Intervals of Median Maths Score",
    subtitle = "Median Point + 95% Quantile Interval",
    x        = "Race",
    y        = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 4: Median and 95% quantile interval of Maths scores by race.





When to use median + qi:

When your data may be skewed (median is more robust than mean).
When you want a distribution-free summary rather than a Normal-theory CI.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
stat_pointinterval() Multiple Widths (95% & 99%)
By passing a vector to .width, we can display both 95% and 99% CIs simultaneously, encoded by line thickness.

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval(
    .width      = c(0.95, 0.99),
    show.legend = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "95% and 99% Confidence Intervals of Mean Maths Score",
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot",
    x        = "Race",
    y        = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 5: Mean with 95% and 99% confidence intervals shown simultaneously.





The thicker inner interval = 95% CI; the thinner outer interval = 99% CI. The 99% interval is always wider, as we need a broader net to be more confident about catching the true mean.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
stat_gradientinterval() Gradient Uncertainty
stat_gradientinterval() encodes probability density through colour opacity: regions of higher likelihood are darker/more opaque, fading toward the tails. This gives a more intuitive sense of where values are most plausible.

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = MATHS)) +
  stat_gradientinterval(
    fill         = "skyblue",
    show.legend  = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Visualising Confidence Intervals of Mean Maths Score",
    subtitle = "Gradient + Interval Plot",
    x        = "Race",
    y        = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 6: Gradient interval plot: opacity encodes density of plausible values.





Why gradient plots are powerful:

They avoid the false binary of “inside the interval = plausible, outside = implausible”.
The continuous fade communicates that probability decreases gradually toward the tails.
Particularly useful when communicating with non-statistical audiences.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
What are HOPs?
A Hypothetical Outcome Plot (HOP) is an animated visualisation that cycles through a set of plausible outcomes drawn from the uncertainty distribution. Rather than showing a static summary (a bar, an interval), it lets the viewer experience the uncertainty through repeated random draws.
Cognitive advantage: Research (Hullman et al., 2015; Kale et al., 2019) shows that people reason better about uncertainty when they see animated samples than when they see static interval representations.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Installing the ungeviz Package
The ungeviz package is not on CRAN and must be installed from GitHub. You only need to do this once.

devtools::install_github("wilkelab/ungeviz")


library(ungeviz)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Building the HOP
The key functions are:

sampler(k, group): draws k bootstrap samples within each group
geom_hpline(): draws a short horizontal line at the sample mean for each draw
transition_states(.draw, ...): animates through draws using gganimate


ggplot(data = exam, 
       (aes(x = factor(RACE), 
            y = MATHS))) +
  geom_point(position = position_jitter(
    height = 0.3, 
    width = 0.05), 
    size = 0.4, 
    color = "#0072B2", 
    alpha = 1/2) +
  geom_hpline(data = sampler(25, 
                             group = RACE), 
              height = 0.6, 
              color = "#D55E00") +
  theme_bw() + 
  transition_states(.draw, 1, 3)







Figure 7: Hypothetical Outcome Plot (HOP): each frame shows a bootstrap sample mean per race group. The animation conveys how much the mean could plausibly vary.





Reading a HOP:

Each frame shows one possible sample mean (orange horizontal line) for each race group.
Groups with high uncertainty (small \(n\), high SD) will show orange lines that jump around a lot between frames.
Groups with low uncertainty will show stable orange lines.
This directly shows the viewer that “the mean could plausibly be anywhere in this range” rather than asking them to interpret an abstract interval statistic.

title: 5a. Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
Two main packages are used:

ggtern: extends ggplot2 to support ternary coordinate systems
plotly: creates interactive web-based charts, its scatterternary trace type renders interactive ternary diagrams

Supporting tidyverse packages (readr, dplyr, tidyr) handle data import and wrangling.

pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)


Note: ggtern masks several ggplot2 functions (e.g. annotate()). Load it after tidyverse to avoid conflicts, and reload ggplot2 functions with ggplot2:: prefix if needed later.

title: 5a. Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
Plotting Ternary Diagrams

Static Ternary Plot - Minimal
ggtern() follows the same grammar as ggplot2 but uses a ternary coordinate system with axes x, y, and z.

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point()




Basic static ternary plot — population structure by subzone, 2018






Static Ternary Plot - Styled
Adding a title and the theme_rgbw() theme colours the axes Red / Green / Blue / White for easier reading.

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title    = "Population Structure by Subzone, 2018",
       subtitle = "Singapore Residents — Young / Active / Old composition",
       x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") +
  theme_rgbw()




Styled static ternary plot with theme_rgbw()





Add-on: Colour-encode a grouping variable
If planning areas (PA) are available after the spread, you can map colour to a categorical variable to reveal spatial clusters:

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD, colour = PA)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Population Structure by Planning Area, 2018") +
  theme_rgbw() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")




Interactive Ternary Plot with Plotly
plot_ly() with type = "scatterternary" renders an interactive version where you can hover to inspect individual subzones.

# Helper: annotation label
label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt,
    x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2
  )
}

# Helper: axis formatting
axis <- function(txt) {
  list(title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10))
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"),
  baxis = axis("Active"),
  caxis = axis("Old")
)

plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated,
  a     = ~YOUNG,
  b     = ~ACTIVE,
  c     = ~OLD,
  color = I("steelblue"),
  type  = "scatterternary",
  mode  = "markers",
  text  = ~paste("Subzone:", SZ, "<br>Total:", TOTAL),  # tooltip add-on
  hoverinfo = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title       = "Population Structure by Subzone, Singapore 2018",
    annotations = label("Ternary Markers"),
    ternary     = ternaryAxes
  )



Interactive ternary plot — hover to see subzone details



Add-on: Colour by population size
Map marker colour to TOTAL population for a richer visual encoding:

plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated,
  a          = ~YOUNG,
  b          = ~ACTIVE,
  c          = ~OLD,
  color      = ~TOTAL,
  colors     = "YlOrRd",
  size       = ~TOTAL,
  sizes      = c(5, 30),
  type       = "scatterternary",
  mode       = "markers",
  text       = ~paste0("Subzone: ", SZ,
                       "<br>Young: ",  scales::percent(YOUNG  / TOTAL, 0.1),
                       "<br>Active: ", scales::percent(ACTIVE / TOTAL, 0.1),
                       "<br>Old: ",    scales::percent(OLD    / TOTAL, 0.1),
                       "<br>Total: ",  formatC(TOTAL, format = "d", big.mark = ",")),
  hoverinfo  = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "Population Structure by Subzone, Singapore 2018",
    ternary = ternaryAxes,
    coloraxis = list(colorscale = "YlOrRd",
                     colorbar   = list(title = "Total Population"))
  )



Interactive ternary plot — colour encodes total population

Key takeaways:

Ternary plots are ideal when three proportions must sum to 1 (or 100%).
ggtern extends the familiar ggplot2 API — most geom_* and theme_* functions still work.
plotly’s scatterternary type adds hover tooltips and zoom for exploratory analysis.
Encoding an additional variable (colour, size) can reveal structure that a plain ternary plot obscures.
